sections.plain_summary: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (hEDS) and Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder (HSD) are conditions where joints move beyond their normal range of motion, often paired with tissue fragility, pain, and dysfunction across multiple body systems. hEDS is a genetic connective tissue disorder; HSD is similar but without the genetic confirmation. Both require long-term management to prevent injury and manage symptoms.
sections.detailed_summary: hEDS and HSD share core features: excessive joint flexibility (hypermobility), chronic pain, and multi-system involvement including skin, digestive, cardiovascular, and neurological symptoms. The distinction hinges on genetics—hEDS is caused by an as-yet-unidentified genetic defect affecting collagen production or structure, while HSD describes the same clinical picture without genetic confirmation. Both are lifelong conditions managed through movement strategy, symptom control, and avoiding further tissue damage. Diagnosis is clinical; there is no definitive genetic test for hEDS despite ongoing research. Symptoms often begin in childhood but may not be recognized until adolescence or adulthood.

prevalence.summary: hEDS is rare; HSD is more common but exact numbers are contested due to evolving diagnostic criteria.
prevalence.estimate: hEDS: approximately 1 in 10,000 to 1 in 30,000 people. HSD: estimated 1–3% of the population, though this figure is debated.
prevalence.notes: hEDS numbers are difficult to pin down because the gene is unknown and diagnosis is clinical. HSD is newer (defined in 2017) and likely includes many previously undiagnosed individuals. Women are diagnosed more often, though whether this reflects true prevalence or diagnostic bias is unclear. Many people with HSD never seek diagnosis.

subgroups.name: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (hEDS)
subgroups.description: The genetic form of the hypermobility disorder. Diagnosis requires hypermobility plus at least two systemic features (skin involvement, unexplained GI symptoms, etc.). Presumed autosomal dominant inheritance, though the causative gene is unknown.
distinguishing_features: Genetic basis presumed but not proven (no test available)
Requires documented family history or multiple systemic features for diagnosis

subgroups.name: Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder (HSD)
subgroups.description: A clinical diagnosis for people with hypermobility and symptoms consistent with hEDS, but who do not meet full criteria or lack family history. Not presumed to have a connective tissue gene defect, though the boundary with hEDS is blurry.
distinguishing_features: May have fewer or milder systemic features than hEDS
Often considered 'generalized hypermobility' plus associated pain or dysfunction
Increasingly recognized as a spectrum rather than a distinct disease

subgroups.name: Secondary Hypermobility
subgroups.description: Joint hypermobility occurring in the context of other conditions (e.g., Marfan syndrome, rheumatologic disease, hyperthyroidism) rather than as a primary connective tissue disorder.
distinguishing_features: Hypermobility is a feature of another diagnosed condition
May resolve if the underlying condition is treated

symptoms.symptom: Joint hypermobility — joints move beyond normal range of motion
symptoms.notes: Often painless when young; worsens with age and activity. Fingers, wrists, knees, shoulders, and hips most commonly affected. Can be measured with the Beighton score.
Often worse in morning or after activity. Worsens over time. Can occur even without obvious injury.

symptoms.symptom: Frequent joint dislocations or subluxations (partial dislocations)
symptoms.notes: May occur spontaneously or with minimal trauma. Some people experience dozens per day; others rarely experience them.
May be generalized or localized. Often paradoxically coincides with hypermobility. Worsens with exertion.
Not always present. Some people have stretchier skin; others have velvety or soft skin. Bruising may be easy or unexplained.

symptoms.symptom: Chronic pain not fully explained by structural injury
symptoms.notes: Often widespread, burning, or neuropathic in character. May be amplified by central sensitization.

symptoms.symptom: Gastrointestinal dysfunction — bloating, constipation, diarrhea, reflux, nausea
symptoms.notes: Can be the most disabling symptom. Often diagnosed as IBS. May include dysmotility and visceral hypersensitivity.

symptoms.symptom: Pelvic floor dysfunction or pelvic pain
symptoms.notes: Common, often under-recognized. May manifest as dyspareunia, urinary urgency, or chronic pelvic pain.
Heart rate jumps on standing, dizziness, chest pain. Often co-occurs with hEDS/HSD but separate diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom: Dysautonomia symptoms — temperature regulation problems, abnormal sweating, blood pressure swings

symptoms.notes: Often related to neck instability or cervical spine issues.

symptoms.symptom: Brain fog, concentration problems, or cognitive fatigue
symptoms.notes: May be related to autonomic dysfunction, pain, or sleep disturbance.

symptoms.symptom: Sleep disturbance — difficulty falling asleep, poor sleep quality, sleep apnea
symptoms.notes: Often multifactorial: pain, anxiety, sleep apnea, or restless legs.
High rates reported; likely related to chronic pain, limited activity, and autonomic dysfunction. Can be primary or secondary.

symptoms.symptom: Sensory sensitivities — light, sound, touch, taste
symptoms.notes: Overlaps with autism and ADHD; may indicate central sensitization.

symptoms.symptom: Temperature intolerance — heat and cold sensitivity
Swelling in joints (especially hands, feet, knees) without inflammation markers
symptoms.notes: May be vasomotor in origin rather than inflammatory.
Related to laryngeal hypermobility or muscle weakness.

key_patterns: Lifelong history of unusual flexibility (often noticed in childhood) plus new onset or progressive pain with aging
Joint pain or dysfunction that doesn't match the severity of findings on imaging or exam
Multiple body system symptoms (joint + GI + autonomic + headache/migraine)
Symptoms worsened by activity but not fully explained by deconditioning
Family history of hypermobility, connective tissue issues, or similar symptoms
Frequent injuries or dislocations from minor trauma or spontaneous incidents
Chronic pain amplified by stress, poor sleep, or flare-up cycles
Symptoms or skin texture changes noticed in adolescence or early adulthood

at_home.name: Beighton Hypermobility Score
at_home.description: Five-part assessment of joint flexibility: (1) Forward bending with knees straight — can you place palms flat on floor? (2) Passive thumb-to-wrist flexion — can thumb touch forearm? (3) Passive finger extension — can fingers bend back more than 90°? (4) Elbow extension past 10° — do elbows hyperextend? (5) Knee extension past 10° — do knees hyperextend? Score 1 point per movement per side (thumb and fingers count as separate). Maximum score is 9. Score of 5+ suggests hypermobility; 4+ plus systemic features is also consistent.
at_home.safety_notes: Do not force movements. Stop if you feel sharp pain or instability. The score decreases with age and is less reliable after age 50. A negative score does not rule out hEDS/HSD, especially in older adults or those with localized hypermobility.

at_home.name: Symptom clustering and family history review
at_home.description: Create a timeline of when joint hypermobility, pain, GI symptoms, and autonomic symptoms began. Note any family members with similar issues, diagnosed connective tissue disorders, chronic pain, or unexplained medical complexity. Early onset (childhood or adolescence) is typical.
at_home.safety_notes: This is informational only and should prompt discussion with a doctor, not be used as diagnosis.

clinical.name: Clinical diagnosis by rheumatologist or EDS specialist
clinical.description: Diagnosis of hEDS is clinical, based on the 2017 International Classification of EDS. Requires hypermobility (Beighton score ≥5 or ≥4 with family history) plus two or more systemic features (skin involvement, GI dysfunction, chronic pain, dysautonomia, easy bruising, joint dislocations, etc.). HSD is diagnosed similarly but without the presumption of a genetic defect. A detailed personal and family history, physical exam, and assessment of multi-system involvement are standard.
clinical.availability: Request referral from primary care doctor to rheumatology or EDS specialist clinic. Many major medical centers have EDS programs. Telemedicine consultations are increasingly available.

clinical.name: Genetic testing (research only)
clinical.description: No single genetic test confirms hEDS; the causative gene(s) remain unknown. Research labs offer panel testing for other EDS types (classic, vascular, kyphoscoliotic) if atypical features are present. Whole-genome or exome sequencing is under investigation but not yet standard of care for hEDS.
clinical.availability: Not routinely available for hEDS diagnosis. May be ordered by a genetic specialist if atypical features suggest another EDS type. Some research programs accept participants.

clinical.name: Skin biopsy for collagen architecture
clinical.description: Electron microscopy can reveal fibril disorganization in hEDS skin, but findings are not diagnostic and not routinely used clinically.
clinical.availability: Research setting only; not recommended for routine diagnosis.

clinical.name: Autonomic function testing (tilt-table test, heart rate variability, sudomotor testing)
clinical.description: Assesses for dysautonomia (including POTS) which commonly co-occurs. Tilt-table test measures heart rate and blood pressure changes on standing. Not specific to hEDS but often needed to evaluate associated symptoms.
clinical.availability: Ordered by cardiologist or autonomic specialist if dysautonomia is suspected. Common in academic medical centers.

clinical.name: Upper endoscopy or other GI imaging
clinical.description: May be ordered to investigate chronic GI symptoms, though imaging is often normal in hEDS despite significant symptoms. Useful to rule out other GI pathology.
clinical.availability: Gastroenterologist referral; ordered as part of workup for unexplained GI symptoms.

clinical.name: Imaging (X-ray, MRI, CT) of symptomatic joints or spine
clinical.description: Often normal or show only minor degenerative changes despite significant pain. Useful to rule out structural injury, instability, or other pathology. Not diagnostic of hEDS.
clinical.availability: Ordered by orthopedic specialist or primary care doctor.

related_conditions.name: Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome (POTS)
related_conditions.explanation: Present in 20–80% of hEDS patients. Heart rate increases excessively on standing, often with dizziness and chest pain. May stem from the same underlying connective tissue defect or dysautonomia.

related_conditions.name: Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS)
related_conditions.explanation: Over-representation in hEDS populations. Mast cells degranulate excessively, causing flushing, GI symptoms, and anaphylaxis. Shared connective tissue basis is proposed.

related_conditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Many hEDS patients are initially diagnosed with IBS. Overlap in bloating, diarrhea, constipation, and abdominal pain. hEDS involves dysmotility and visceral hypersensitivity; IBS lacks the multi-system hypermobility features.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: Central sensitization and amplified pain occur in both. Fibromyalgia does not include hypermobility or connective tissue features; hEDS does. Some individuals have both diagnoses.

related_conditions.name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome / Myalgic Encephalomyelitis (ME/CFS)
related_conditions.explanation: Post-exertional malaise, autonomic dysfunction, and central sensitization overlap. Some hEDS patients meet ME/CFS criteria. Shared biological mechanisms are being investigated.

related_conditions.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) and ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: Emerging evidence suggests higher prevalence of autism and ADHD in hEDS cohorts. Sensory sensitivities and autonomic dysfunction overlap. Genetic and neurobiological connections are under study.

related_conditions.name: Cervical spine instability
related_conditions.explanation: hEDS affects cervical ligaments and discs, leading to instability. Can cause headaches, neck pain, and neurological symptoms. May require imaging and specialist evaluation.

related_conditions.name: Temporomandibular Joint (TMJ) Disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Hypermobility of the jaw joint is common in hEDS, causing pain, clicking, limited opening, or chronic tension.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Connective tissue defects and muscle weakness in hEDS extend to pelvic floor, causing dyspareunia, urinary symptoms, or pelvic pain.

related_conditions.name: Marfan Syndrome or other genetic EDS subtypes
related_conditions.explanation: Other EDS types (classic, vascular, kyphoscoliotic) have different genetic causes and clinical features. Vascular EDS is life-threatening and requires different management. Genetic testing may be needed to differentiate.

strategies.name: Activity pacing and energy envelope management
strategies.description: Stay within a sustainable activity level to avoid post-activity crashes. Track energy expenditure (physical, cognitive, emotional) and build rest into schedules. Aim for consistency rather than 'good' and 'bad' days. This prevents the flare-crash cycle that worsens symptoms over time.
strategies.notes: Critical to avoid post-exertional malaise. Requires honest self-monitoring and willingness to do less on good days. Differs from conventional 'exercise as much as tolerated' advice.

strategies.name: Low-impact or isometric exercise and physical therapy
strategies.description: Strengthen muscles around hypermobile joints without destabilizing them. Isometric holds, swimming, water aerobics, Pilates, or gentle yoga with modifications. Avoid ballistic stretching and hyperextension. Work with a PT experienced in EDS to avoid harmful exercises.
strategies.notes: Strength reduces pain and injury risk. Avoid 'pushing through' pain or fatigue. Consistency matters more than intensity. Many generic exercise programs worsen hEDS.

strategies.name: Joint stabilization and proprioceptive training
strategies.description: Use of compression garments (sleeves, wraps, or braces) to provide external feedback and stability. Proprioceptive training (balance work, positional awareness) helps the nervous system compensate for joint laxity.
strategies.notes: Compression gloves, knee sleeves, or ankle braces can reduce pain and joint stress. Kinesiology tape is often used but evidence is mixed. Custom bracing may be needed for severe instability.

strategies.name: Heat and cold therapy
strategies.description: Heat (warm baths, heating pads) loosens tight muscles and improves blood flow. Cold reduces acute inflammation after activity or injury. Use cautiously in autonomic dysfunction (heat may worsen POTS).
strategies.notes: Heat is often more helpful for chronic pain; cold for acute injury. Individual response varies. Test cautiously if dysautonomia is present.

strategies.name: Sleep optimization
strategies.description: Consistent sleep schedule, dark and cool bedroom, supportive pillows, and avoiding stimulants 6+ hours before bed. Sleep quality directly impacts pain, cognition, and immune function.
strategies.notes: Sleep disturbance is common; addressing it is foundational. May require sleep study to rule out sleep apnea.

strategies.name: Stress reduction and nervous system regulation
strategies.description: Meditation, deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, or gentle yoga help downregulate the autonomic nervous system. Chronic stress amplifies pain and dysautonomia.
strategies.notes: Parasympathetic activation (via vagus nerve) can reduce pain flares and improve GI function.

strategies.name: Dietary management for GI symptoms
strategies.description: Low-FODMAP diet, elimination diet, or small frequent meals may help if IBS-like symptoms are present. Avoid trigger foods (often spicy, fatty, high-sugar). Stay hydrated and consider soluble fiber.
strategies.notes: GI symptoms are highly individual. Low-FODMAP has evidence for IBS and may help hEDS patients. Trial and track what works. Some patients benefit from digestive enzymes or prokinetic agents.

strategies.name: Hydration and electrolyte balance
strategies.description: Drink adequate water (especially if dysautonomia is present) and consider electrolyte supplementation if POTS or other autonomic symptoms exist. Dehydration worsens dysautonomia and pain.
strategies.notes: Higher-than-typical fluid intake often needed, especially with POTS. Electrolyte drinks (e.g., coconut water, sports drinks) may be helpful.

strategies.name: Anti-inflammatory diet or nutritional support
strategies.description: Omega-3 fatty acids, antioxidant-rich foods, and whole foods may help reduce systemic inflammation and support connective tissue. Some practitioners recommend vitamin C, copper, or other micronutrient support, though evidence is limited.
strategies.notes: No single diet is proven for hEDS. Whole-food, nutrient-dense eating is generally advised. Supplement with caution and under professional guidance.

strategies.name: Pain management — non-opioid pharmacotherapy
strategies.description: NSAIDs (if tolerated), acetaminophen, or low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline) for neuropathic pain. Gabapentin or pregabalin may help neuropathic or centralized pain. Muscle relaxants sometimes used short-term.
strategies.notes: No medications specifically target hEDS. Pain management is often multi-modal. NSAIDs carry GI and cardiovascular risks in hEDS. Opioids are generally avoided due to habit-forming risk and GI side effects.

strategies.name: Autonomic symptom management (POTS, dysautonomia)
strategies.description: Beta-blockers, midodrine, or fludrocortisone may be prescribed by a cardiologist or autonomic specialist. Compression garments, increased salt and fluid intake, and head-of-bed elevation also help.
strategies.notes: Pharmacotherapy is individualized. POTS in hEDS is high-prevalence and often requires specific treatment.

strategies.name: GI prokinetics and medications
strategies.description: Metoclopramide or domperidone may help if gastroparesis is present. Antacids, H2-blockers, or proton-pump inhibitors for reflux. Stool softeners or osmotic laxatives for constipation.
strategies.notes: GI dysfunction is multifactorial (dysmotility, hypersensitivity, dysbiosis). Medications are adjunctive to dietary and lifestyle changes.

strategies.name: Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) and psychological support
strategies.description: CBT can address pain catastrophizing, anxiety, and depression. Regular therapy or counseling normalizes the condition and helps develop coping strategies.
strategies.notes: Mental health is integral to hEDS management. Therapists experienced with chronic illness are most helpful.

strategies.name: Occupational therapy and ergonomic modification
strategies.description: Optimize workspace, use adaptive equipment, and develop daily routines that minimize joint stress. Modifying activities of daily living reduces pain and injury.
strategies.notes: Occupational therapists trained in EDS can provide tailored recommendations.

strategies.name: Pelvic floor physical therapy
strategies.description: Specialist PT for pelvic floor dysfunction, dyspareunia, or urinary symptoms. May include relaxation techniques, biofeedback, or gentle strengthening.
strategies.notes: Many people with hEDS benefit from pelvic floor PT. Requires a PT trained in hypermobility disorders.

strategies.name: Supplements — vitamin C and copper
strategies.description: Vitamin C is important for collagen synthesis; copper is a cofactor for lysyl oxidase (collagen cross-linking). Some practitioners recommend supplementation, but evidence for hEDS-specific benefit is anecdotal.
strategies.notes: Supplementation is not standard of care and should be discussed with a practitioner. High-dose vitamin C can cause kidney stones; monitor copper-zinc balance.

strategies.name: Supplements — omega-3 fatty acids
strategies.description: May reduce systemic inflammation and support cellular health. Often recommended as part of nutritional support for chronic pain.
strategies.notes: Some evidence from general chronic pain literature but not EDS-specific.

things_to_avoid: High-intensity exercise, ballistic stretching, or 'pushing through pain' — worsens joint damage and flare cycles
Rapid position changes or activities that jar the spine (jumping, running) — increases injury and dislocation risk
Extended immobility or deconditioning — increases pain and disability over time
Overuse of NSAIDs — GI complications, renal risk, and worsening dysautonomia
Opioid pain medications — habit-forming, GI side effects, tolerance develops
High-dose vitamin C supplementation — kidney stone risk, can worsen some symptoms if excessive
Extreme heat exposure — can worsen dysautonomia and POTS symptoms
Starvation diets or restriction — impairs muscle and connective tissue health
Isolation or avoiding all activity — worsens depression, deconditioning, and long-term outcomes
Practitioners unfamiliar with EDS — may recommend exercises or treatments that cause harm

key_levers.lever: Accurate diagnosis and education about the condition
key_levers.why_it_matters: Many hEDS patients are misdiagnosed for years (as anxiety, malingering, fibromyalgia, IBS, etc.), delaying effective management and increasing disability. Understanding what you have enables informed self-care and prevents harmful advice.
key_levers.how_to_start: Request a referral to a rheumatologist or EDS specialist. Bring a symptom timeline and family history. Read foundational resources (Ehlers-Danlos Society, peer-reviewed reviews).
key_levers.timeline: Diagnosis can take months; relief from validation is often immediate. Practical improvement follows once you understand your triggers and limits.

key_levers.lever: Activity pacing and energy envelope management
key_levers.why_it_matters: The flare-crash cycle is the primary driver of long-term disability. Staying within a sustainable energy envelope prevents crashes and gradually increases tolerance.
key_levers.how_to_start: For one week, track activities (physical, cognitive, emotional), energy level (0–10), and symptoms next day. Identify your 'threshold.' Plan days to stay slightly below that threshold.
key_levers.timeline: Weeks to months for significant reduction in flare frequency. Long-term improvement is substantial.

key_levers.lever: Targeted physical therapy with an EDS-aware practitioner
key_levers.why_it_matters: Appropriate strengthening and proprioceptive training improve function and reduce pain. Generic PT can cause harm; specialist knowledge is critical.
key_levers.how_to_start: Ask your rheumatologist for a PT referral (preferably someone trained in hypermobility). Start with 1–2 sessions per week of gentle, isometric work.
key_levers.timeline: Noticeable improvement in 4–8 weeks with consistency. Long-term gains accumulate over months and years.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Poor sleep amplifies pain, worsens dysautonomia, and impairs recovery. Improving sleep is foundational and often yields rapid benefit.
key_levers.how_to_start: Set a consistent bedtime and wake time. Remove screens 1 hour before bed. Use blackout curtains and a cool room (60–67°F). Track sleep quality for 2 weeks.
key_levers.timeline: Some improvement in 1–2 weeks; significant benefit in 4+ weeks with consistency.

key_levers.lever: Management of comorbid autonomic dysfunction (POTS, dysautonomia)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Autonomic dysfunction amplifies pain, fatigue, and GI symptoms. Treating it reduces overall symptom burden significantly.
key_levers.how_to_start: If dizziness or tachycardia on standing is present, ask for cardiology referral and consider a tilt-table test. Increase salt and fluid intake as a first-line intervention.
key_levers.timeline: Weeks to months depending on severity and medication response.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic GI symptoms often cause as much disability as joint pain. Improving GI function improves nutrition, energy, and quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Gastroenterology referral for evaluation. Trial of low-FODMAP diet or elimination diet. Small frequent meals, hydration, and consideration of prokinetics.
key_levers.timeline: 4–8 weeks for dietary changes; 2–4 weeks for medication response.

key_levers.lever: Mental health support and stress reduction
key_levers.why_it_matters: Depression and anxiety are common and amplify pain and dysautonomia. Addressing them improves overall outcomes and quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss mood and anxiety with primary care doctor or therapist. Consider CBT or mindfulness-based approaches. Join a patient community.
key_levers.timeline: Benefits accumulate over weeks and months with consistent engagement.

common_mistakes: Pushing through pain or ignoring early warning signs — escalates flares and long-term disability. hEDS requires early intervention to prevent activity flares.
Following generic exercise advice (stretch daily, no pain no gain) — can cause dislocations or tissue damage in hEDS. Specialist guidance is essential.
Accepting isolation or bed rest — worsens deconditioning and depression. Appropriate, paced activity is necessary.
Ignoring or minimizing GI symptoms — they often drive the greatest disability and require separate specialist attention.
Not addressing comorbid autonomic dysfunction — dysautonomia amplifies pain and fatigue; treating it has outsized benefit.
Stopping all medication or supplements abruptly — can lead to symptom rebound or flares. Changes should be gradual and monitored.
Relying on a single provider or approach — hEDS requires multi-disciplinary care (rheumatology, PT, cardiology, GI, mental health, etc.).
Catastrophizing or defining yourself entirely by the diagnosis — hEDS is real and limiting, but many people maintain meaningful quality of life with proper management.

specialists.role: Often the primary diagnoser of hEDS; evaluates hypermobility, systemic features, and multi-system involvement. Can coordinate overall care and rule out autoimmune or inflammatory conditions.
specialists.notes: Not all rheumatologists are familiar with hEDS/HSD; seek someone with specific interest or training. Some centers have dedicated EDS clinics.

specialists.role: Evaluates for genetic connective tissue disorders, reviews family history, and may order genetic testing for other EDS types or related conditions.
specialists.notes: Helpful if diagnosis is unclear or if atypical features suggest a different EDS type.

specialists.role: Designs tailored strengthening, proprioceptive training, and movement strategies. Critical for preventing injury and improving function.
specialists.notes: Many PTs lack hEDS training; seek specific certification or experience. Bad PT can cause serious harm.

specialists.role: Addresses activities of daily living, ergonomic modifications, energy management, and adaptive strategies to reduce pain and injury.
specialists.notes: Often overlooked but highly valuable for practical quality-of-life improvements.

specialists.role: Diagnoses and manages POTS, dysautonomia, and other cardiovascular complications. Orders tilt-table testing and prescribes autonomic medications.
specialists.notes: Essential if dysautonomia symptoms are present. May be based at major academic medical center.

specialists.role: Evaluates and manages GI dysmotility, reflux, and visceral hypersensitivity. Orders imaging or motility studies if needed.
specialists.notes: GI dysfunction is common and often the most disabling symptom; specialist attention is important.

specialists.role: Specializes in pelvic floor dysfunction, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms, and pelvic pain related to hypermobility.
specialists.notes: Significantly under-recognized; many hEDS patients benefit from pelvic floor PT.

specialists.type: Psychologist or therapist with chronic illness experience
specialists.role: Provides CBT, coping strategies, anxiety and depression management, and emotional support for living with chronic conditions.
specialists.notes: Integral to comprehensive care. Avoid therapists who minimize the physical reality of the condition.

specialists.role: May take a systems-based approach to symptom management, nutritional support, and lifestyle optimization. Not always familiar with EDS.
specialists.notes: Can be helpful for supplement and dietary guidance, but should complement (not replace) conventional diagnosis and management.

specialists.role: Evaluates and treats sleep apnea, insomnia, and other sleep disorders common in hEDS.
specialists.notes: Consider if sleep disturbance is severe or unresponsive to basic sleep hygiene.

specialists.role: Evaluates structural instability or severe joint damage; may recommend bracing or rarely surgical intervention.
specialists.notes: Surgery is generally a last resort in hEDS due to tissue fragility and poor healing. Seek someone with EDS experience.

patient_communities.name: Ehlers-Danlos Society
patient_communities.platform: Website with resources, support groups, provider directory
patient_communities.notes: Official nonprofit organization. Provides educational resources, patient symposiums, research updates, and a specialist provider directory.

patient_communities.name: The Ehlers-Danlos Society — Support Groups
patient_communities.notes: Peer support by condition and region. Helpful for shared experience and practical tips.

patient_communities.name: r/ehlersdanlos
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community with daily discussions, resource sharing, and peer support. Good for quick questions and validation.

patient_communities.name: Hypermobility Syndromes UK
patient_communities.platform: Website with resources and community forum
patient_communities.notes: UK-based but international reach. Comprehensive resources on HSD and hEDS.

patient_communities.name: EDS Facebook Support Groups
patient_communities.notes: Many condition-specific and region-specific Facebook groups exist. Often more intimate than Reddit; moderated peer support.

patient_communities.name: POTS Dysautonomia Support Network
patient_communities.notes: If autonomic dysfunction is a major symptom, this community provides specialist support and resources.

patient_communities.name: IBS and Digestive Health Communities
patient_communities.notes: If GI symptoms are predominant, cross-over communities on IBS often include hEDS members and dietary/lifestyle strategies.

patient_communities.name: Patient-led EDS Discord servers
patient_communities.notes: Private Discord communities organized by condition and interest (e.g., hEDS, HSD, parents of children with EDS). Real-time chat and smaller groups.

learning_resources.title: The Ehlers-Danlos Syndromes: Clinical Manifestations and Management (book)
learning_resources.description: The definitive clinical reference for EDS. Detailed, evidence-based, and written for healthcare providers but readable for motivated patients. Covers genetics, diagnosis, complications, and management.

learning_resources.title: Living Life to the Fullest with EDS
learning_resources.description: Patient-focused guide covering daily life, activity management, and practical strategies. Accessible and action-oriented.

learning_resources.title: International Classification of the Ehlers-Danlos Syndromes (2017)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Malfait et al., American Journal of Medical Genetics
learning_resources.description: The diagnostic criteria for hEDS and HSD. Dense but the gold standard for clinicians and informed patients. Explains the reclassification and current understanding.

learning_resources.title: The EDS Information Center (website)
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive, regularly updated information on all EDS types, including fact sheets, provider directories, webinars, and research updates.

learning_resources.title: Managing hEDS and HSD — A Patient's Guide to Symptom Control and Movement (online course)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Hypermobility Syndromes UK or The Ehlers-Danlos Society
learning_resources.description: Educational videos covering symptom management, activity pacing, exercise principles, and daily living strategies.

learning_resources.title: Hypermobility, Fibromyalgia and the Chronic Pain Syndromes (book)
learning_resources.description: Bridges hypermobility and pain science. Explains the overlaps and differences. Practical movement and pain management strategies.

learning_resources.title: The Body Keeps the Score (book)
learning_resources.description: While not EDS-specific, this book on trauma and the nervous system is highly relevant for understanding how chronic pain and dysautonomia affect the body and brain. Helps explain multi-system symptoms.

learning_resources.title: Pacing Guide and Energy Envelope Approach
learning_resources.author_or_source: Action for M.E. / chronic illness communities
learning_resources.description: Detailed explanation of the energy envelope and pacing principles critical to hEDS management. Originally developed for ME/CFS but applicable across conditions.

learning_resources.title: POTS and Dysautonomia Resources
learning_resources.description: Specialist resources on POTS and other autonomic disorders. Includes diagnostic criteria, treatment guidelines, and patient education.

learning_resources.title: Low-FODMAP Diet for IBS (book or app)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Sue Shepherd, Peter Gibson (or Monash University app)
learning_resources.description: Evidence-based dietary approach for GI symptoms. Relevant for many hEDS patients with IBS-like symptoms. Requires care and often dietitian guidance.

learning_resources.title: Research Literature on hEDS and HSD
learning_resources.description: Search terms: 'hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos', 'hypermobility spectrum disorder', 'connective tissue disorder', 'dysautonomia EDS'. Peer-reviewed research on epidemiology, diagnosis, and management.

learning_resources.title: YouTube Channels on EDS and Hypermobility
learning_resources.author_or_source: EDS Healthcare Professionals, EDS patient educators
learning_resources.description: Educational videos on movement, exercises, symptom management, and living with hEDS. Quality varies; seek channels endorsed by the Ehlers-Danlos Society.

learning_resources.title: Podcasts on Chronic Illness and EDS
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various patient advocates and healthcare providers
learning_resources.description: Podcasts such as 'The Connective Tissue Podcast' or chronic illness-focused shows explore EDS, symptom management, and patient experiences. Accessible, conversational format.

sections.relevance_signal: **Joints that bend further than normal**, frequent sprains, stretchy skin. Gut symptoms alongside joint pain, fatigue, and POTS or MCAS. Multi-system involvement.
